does: This paper proposes DEPO (data-dependent exploration for preference optimization), an online RLHF algorithm that uses historical preference data to construct uncertainty bonuses that encourage exploration in high-uncertainty regions. The method leverages offline data statistics to derive exploration bonuses, avoiding the need to estimate on-policy expectations from limited historical feedback. The core contribution is a scalable approach to preference optimization that adaptively weights exploration based on data-dependent uncertainty estimates, with a theoretical regret bound demonstrating adaptation to problem hardness and convergence guarantees under reasonable assumptions about the preference model.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of online RLHF for LLM alignment, which extends classical reinforcement learning to the human-in-the-loop setting where preference labels are continuously collected during training. It responds to known limitations of existing exploration strategies in preference-based learning, which often rely on on-policy bonus estimation—a technique that becomes unreliable with sparse preference data and limited exploration history. The paper connects to broader exploration-exploitation research in RL and recent advances in uncertainty quantification for preference models, combining insights from bandit algorithms and offline RL to address the specific challenge of identifying informative preference pairs during online training.
enables: This work enables more sample-efficient online RLHF systems that can better balance exploration and exploitation when learning from human feedback. By using data-dependent uncertainty to guide exploration, the approach opens pathways toward LLM alignment methods that require fewer human preference labels while discovering higher-quality behavioral policies. The theoretical framework potentially extends to other preference-based learning problems and could inform exploration strategies in multi-round interactive learning scenarios. Future work might incorporate the method into end-to-end LLM training pipelines, investigate tighter regret bounds, or apply similar data-dependent exploration principles to other alignment paradigms involving limited evaluator feedback.
